title: Pork & Fennel Ragù

overview.description: A slow-simmered Italian pork ragù with fennel, white wine and a touch of tomato, finished with milk for a rich, silky sauce.

method: Heat the olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat.
Cook the onion, carrot, celery and fennel for 10 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic and crushed fennel seeds and cook for 1 minute.
Increase the heat and add the pork mince.
Cook until well browned, breaking it into small pieces.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes.
Pour in the white wine and reduce by half.
Add the chopped tomatos, chicken stock and bay leaves.
Cook uncovered for 1½–2 hours, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the milk during the final 10 minutes.
Taste and season with salt and black pepper.
Remove the bay leaves before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Many Italian ragùs use surprisingly little tomato. The meat, wine and slow cooking provide most of the flavour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build flavour by browning meat properly and gently reducing a slow-cooked ragù.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A freezer-friendly pasta sauce that's inexpensive, comforting and a nice alternative to beef ragù.
chapman-tips: Take time to brown the pork well—it adds a huge amount of flavour.
Lightly crushing the fennel seeds releases their aroma.
The milk rounds out the sauce and softens the acidity.
This tastes even better the following day.
Finish cooking the pasta in the ragù with a splash of pasta water for 1–2 minutes. The starch helps the sauce cling to every strand instead of sitting underneath it.
notes.batch-cooking: Double the recipe and freeze in family-sized portions.
notes.leftovers: Excellent in lasagne, on baked potatoes or stirred through gnocchi.
